title: Fix UsageGate race condition and _fire_cost_event robustness
analysis:
UsageGate.before_invoke() has a race condition in its failover path (lines 178-187): _last_account_name is updated AFTER 'await _write_cost_event()', creating a window where concurrent callers see stale state. Additionally, the await blocks the critical path of returning an OAuth token for a telemetry-only write.

_fire_cost_event() (lines 316-332) has three robustness issues: (1) the Task returned by loop.create_task() is not stored, so it may be garbage-collected before completion; (2) 'except RuntimeError: pass' catches ALL RuntimeErrors, not just the 'no running event loop' error from get_running_loop(); (3) errors are silently swallowed with no logging.

Finally, json.dumps() is called unconditionally in _handle_cap_detected() (line 264) and _account_resume_probe_loop() (line 410) even when _cost_store is None, doing unnecessary serialization work.

Existing test patterns: pytest-asyncio with @pytest.mark.asyncio class decorators, unittest.mock for patching, conftest.py ensures local src takes precedence. No existing test file for usage_gate — we will create shared/tests/test_usage_gate.py.

The UsageGate constructor reads account tokens from env vars, so tests will use patch.dict(os.environ) to inject test tokens. For unit tests targeting internal methods, we can also set _accounts directly.

prerequisites.description: Create shared/tests/test_usage_gate.py with imports, helper fixtures (make_gate factory that creates a UsageGate with patched env vars and configurable accounts/cost_store), and pytest-asyncio configuration

steps.description: Write failing tests for before_invoke race condition: (a) _last_account_name is already updated to the new account's name at the moment the failover cost event fires, (b) failover event uses _fire_cost_event (fire-and-forget) not await _write_cost_event (blocking). Test by mocking _fire_cost_event with a side_effect that captures gate._last_account_name at call time, setting up 2 accounts with account A capped, gate._last_account_name='acct-A', then calling before_invoke(). Assert captured name == 'acct-B' and _fire_cost_event was called (not _write_cost_event).

Fix before_invoke() failover path: save old_name = self._last_account_name, then set self._last_account_name = acct.name BEFORE the event, then use self._fire_cost_event() instead of await self._write_cost_event(). This eliminates both the race window and the blocking await.

Write failing test for _fire_cost_event task reference storage: create a gate with a mock cost_store, call _fire_cost_event inside a running event loop, assert the task is stored in gate._background_tasks. Also test that after the task completes (await pending tasks), the set is empty again (done-callback cleanup).
Add self._background_tasks: set[asyncio.Task] = set() to __init__. In _fire_cost_event, store the task reference and add a done_callback that calls self._background_tasks.discard(task).

Write failing tests for narrowed RuntimeError handling in _fire_cost_event: (a) when asyncio.get_running_loop() raises RuntimeError('no running event loop'), the exception is caught and a warning is logged (use caplog fixture); (b) when loop.create_task() raises RuntimeError('some other error'), the exception is NOT caught — it propagates. Currently both are silently caught by the broad except.

Restructure _fire_cost_event: wrap only asyncio.get_running_loop() in try/except RuntimeError with logger.warning('No running event loop for cost event %s/%s', event_type, account_name). Move loop.create_task() and task storage outside the try block so its errors propagate normally.

Write failing test for json.dumps guard in _handle_cap_detected: create a gate with cost_store=None, mock json.dumps, call _handle_cap_detected with a reason string. Assert json.dumps was NOT called for the cost event serialization. Verify _fire_cost_event was NOT called. (Currently json.dumps is called unconditionally on line 264.)
Guard line 264 in _handle_cap_detected: wrap the _fire_cost_event call (and its json.dumps argument) with 'if self._cost_store:'.

Write failing test for json.dumps guard in _account_resume_probe_loop: create a gate with cost_store=None, one capped account with resets_at in the past, run the probe loop. Assert _write_cost_event was NOT called (currently it's called unconditionally on line 410, though it returns early). Use mock on _write_cost_event to verify it's never reached.
Guard line 410 in _account_resume_probe_loop: wrap the await self._write_cost_event call (and its json.dumps argument) with 'if self._cost_store:'.

design_decisions.decision: Use a set with done-callback for background task storage rather than a list or WeakSet
design_decisions.rationale: set + discard-on-done is the standard asyncio pattern for preventing GC of fire-and-forget tasks. WeakSet would defeat the purpose (allowing GC). A list would grow unboundedly without cleanup. The done_callback with discard is O(1) and self-cleaning.

design_decisions.decision: Narrow the RuntimeError catch by restructuring the try block rather than inspecting the exception message
design_decisions.rationale: Checking exception messages is fragile and locale-dependent. Instead, we put only asyncio.get_running_loop() inside the try/except, and leave loop.create_task() outside. This is structurally correct: get_running_loop() is the only call that raises RuntimeError for 'no current event loop'.

design_decisions.decision: Use 'if self._cost_store' guard at call sites rather than relying on early-return inside _fire_cost_event/_write_cost_event
design_decisions.rationale: The early-return already exists inside those methods, but the json.dumps() call at the call site runs unconditionally before the method is entered. Guarding at the call site avoids the unnecessary serialization and makes the no-cost-store path zero-cost.

design_decisions.decision: Capture old_name in a local variable before updating _last_account_name
design_decisions.rationale: We need the previous account name for the failover event payload ('from' field), but _last_account_name must be updated before the event fires. Storing old_name in a local variable cleanly separates the concerns.

design_decisions.decision: Do not modify _start_account_resume_probe's similar broad except RuntimeError pattern
design_decisions.rationale: The task description scopes the exception narrowing to _fire_cost_event (lines 316-332) only. _start_account_resume_probe stores the task in acct.resume_task (not GC-vulnerable) and its RuntimeError catch is functionally equivalent. Staying within scope to avoid unplanned churn.

reuse.how: Create UsageCapConfig(accounts=[AccountConfig(name=..., oauth_token_env=...)]) to instantiate gates with controlled account configurations

reuse.where: The fire-and-forget wrapper _fire_cost_event delegates to it
reuse.how: _fire_cost_event already calls _write_cost_event; no new write path needed. The early-return in _write_cost_event provides defense-in-depth.

reuse.where: test_usage_gate.py for mocking env vars and methods
reuse.how: Use patch.dict(os.environ, {...}) for account token injection, patch.object for method interception

reuse.what: pytest-asyncio @pytest.mark.asyncio class decorator pattern from test_cost_store.py
reuse.how: Decorate test classes with @pytest.mark.asyncio, use async def test methods
